title: The Ajna Chakra and Kundalini: Third Eye Opening Without Losing Your Mind
meta_description: You're here, aren't you? It's probably late. You're probably scared. You've felt something, seen something, heard something that doesn't fit into your old
body:
The Ajna Chakra and Kundalini: Third Eye Opening Without Losing Your Mind

You're here, aren't you? It's probably late. You're probably scared. You've felt something, seen something, heard something that doesn't fit into your old world. Maybe it started subtle, a flicker at the edge of your vision, a strange knowing, a pressure between your brows. Now it's not so subtle. It's a roar, a scream, a relentless current that feels like it's tearing you apart. You're wondering if you're losing it. I know that feeling. I've been there. I'm Kalesh, and I survived what they call a kundalini crisis. I'm not going to sugarcoat this for you. What you're experiencing, it's real. And it can be terrifying. But you're not alone, and you're not going crazy. Not in the way you think, anyway.

Let's talk about the Ajna chakra, the so-called "third eye." It's not some mystical woo-woo thing; it's a center of consciousness. And when kundalini energy starts moving, especially if it's not doing it gently, this area can become a hotspot of intense activity. You're not imagining the pressure, the buzzing, the lights, the visions. It's all part of the process. But here's the thing: you don't have to be completely overwhelmed by it. You can learn to work with it, to integrate it, without feeling like your mind is shattering into a thousand pieces. That's the goal here. That's what I want to help you understand.

You might be having visions, hearing voices, feeling sensations that are utterly alien. You might even feel like you're being attacked, or that something is taking over your body. I've heard it all, and honestly, I've felt a lot of it myself. The key is to understand what's happening, to ground yourself, and to create a container for this immense energy. Because that's what it is - pure, raw energy. And it needs direction, not just a free-for-all in your head. Right?

What's Happening "Up There"?

So, you're feeling things in your head, right? Between your eyebrows, maybe behind them, a kind of pressure, throbbing, buzzing, or even a sense of something opening. This is often associated with the Ajna chakra, sometimes called the third eye. It's not just a poetic idea; it's a recognized energy center in many traditions. When kundalini energy rises, it often makes its way through the central channel of your body, and the Ajna is a major stop. It's like a high-voltage junction box for consciousness. When it activates, things get intense.

You might be seeing flashes of light, geometric patterns, colors you've never seen before. You might get sudden insights, a knowing that feels like it bypasses your ordinary thought process. Or, it might be much more dramatic: full-blown visions, auditory hallucinations, a sense of other presences. For some, it's like a psychic switch gets flipped and they're suddenly overwhelmed with information, other people's thoughts, or glimpses into other realms. It can be exhilarating, but it can also be absolutely terrifying, especially if you have no context for it. Know what I mean? You're sitting there, trying to watch TV, and suddenly you're seeing spirits or hearing voices. It's disorienting, to say the least.

The brain itself is undergoing changes. Neuroplasticity isn't just a buzzword; it's what happens when new connections form, when old ones get rewired. Kundalini can be a catalyst for rapid, sometimes chaotic, neuroplastic changes. Your nervous system is recalibrating, often at warp speed. That's why you feel so wired, so sensitive, so utterly exposed. Your sensory filters are thinning, and you're perceiving more than you ever have before. It's like turning up the volume on reality to eleven, and you didn't even know there was an eleven. This isn't a sign of madness; it's often a sign of deep spiritual awakening, albeit a very messy one.

The Overload Factor

Here's the rub: your system wasn't designed for this kind of input all at once. It's like trying to download a super-high-definition movie onto a floppy disk. Your brain, your nervous system, your entire being is trying to process an incredible amount of data. This overload can manifest as extreme anxiety, paranoia, panic attacks, even temporary psychosis. You might feel disconnected from your body, like you're floating, or conversely, feel trapped and overwhelmed within it. Sleep often goes out the window, which only makes everything worse. You're exhausted, wired, and your mind is racing, cycling through intense experiences. It's a vicious cycle, and it's why so many people end up in crisis. I've been there, staring at the ceiling at 3 AM, convinced I was losing my grip on reality. The world felt like it was breaking apart, and I was breaking with it. But you're not breaking; you're expanding. It just feels like breaking because it's so unfamiliar and so intense. Does that land?

When to Seek Help (No Shame in It)

Look, I'm not a doctor, and I can't give you medical advice. But I can tell you this: if you're experiencing severe distress, if you feel like you're a danger to yourself or others, or if you can't distinguish reality from hallucination, you need to get professional help. There's absolutely no shame in it. Sometimes, psychiatric medication can provide a temporary buffer, a way to dial down the intensity enough for you to get some rest and regain your footing. It's not "shutting down" your awakening; it's creating a safe space for it to continue in a more manageable way. I've seen too many people try to white-knuckle it and end up in a much worse place. You need to be able to function, to eat, to sleep. If you can't do that, get help. Find a therapist who understands spiritual emergence, if you can. The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) is a brilliant book that really speaks to this process, and it might help you find a therapist who actually gets it.

Grounding: Your Lifeline

When your head feels like it's going to explode, the absolute most important thing you can do is ground yourself. This isn't just some spiritual platitude; it's a survival strategy. You're getting too much energy "up top," and you need to bring it down, distribute it throughout your body, and connect with the earth. Think of it like a lightning rod. If you don't have one, the lightning just fries everything. You need to channel that energy. Here's how:

  • Connect with Nature: Get outside. Walk barefoot on the grass, touch a tree, sit by the ocean, feel the sun on your skin. Physically connect with the earth. It sounds simple, but it's incredibly effective. The earth is a massive grounding force. Let it absorb some of that excess energy.
  • Physical Activity: Move your body. Not just gentle stretching, but something that gets you sweating, that connects you to your physical form. Running, dancing, yoga, martial arts - anything that makes you feel your body, feel your breath. This helps move stagnant or overwhelming energy out of your head and through your limbs.
  • Earthy Foods: Eat root vegetables, heavy grains, warm, cooked meals. Avoid stimulants like caffeine and sugar. Your body needs dense, nourishing food to anchor you. Think about what makes you feel heavy and settled, not light and airy.
  • Sensory Focus: Deliberately engage your five senses. What do you see right now? What do you hear? What do you smell? Taste? Touch? Focus intensely on one sense at a time. This pulls your awareness out of your head and into your present physical reality.
  • Deep Breathing: Slow, deep breaths, especially focusing on exhaling fully. Imagine you're breathing down into your belly, into your legs, into the earth. Breathing is your anchor. It's always there, always available.
  • Cold Showers/Baths: This is a shock to the system, but it can be incredibly grounding. The cold forces you into your body, into the present moment. It's like hitting a reset button for your nervous system.

You're probably thinking, "But I can't do any of that, I'm too overwhelmed!" I know. Start small. Even five minutes outside. Even one deep breath. Every little bit helps. The point isn't to stop the process, but to manage its intensity so you don't completely lose your footing. You need to create a sense of safety within your own body, even when your mind feels like a wild storm. For managing the sheer intensity of anxiety that comes with this, I'd suggest looking into The Mindfulness and Acceptance Workbook for Anxiety (paid link). It's not specifically for kundalini, but the techniques for coping with overwhelming sensations are incredibly useful.

Integration, Not Suppression

A lot of people, when they go through this, they just want it to stop. They want to go back to "normal." I get it. Believe me, I do. But you can't un-ring this bell. This isn't something you can just shut off or ignore. The energy is moving, and it will keep moving. Trying to suppress it is like trying to hold a beach ball underwater - it's exhausting, and eventually, it's going to pop up with even more force. The goal isn't to suppress it; it's to integrate it. It's to learn how to live with this expanded awareness, this heightened sensitivity, this new level of energy.

Integration means making space for these experiences without letting them completely derail your life. It means finding ways to process the information you're receiving, to understand the visions, to interpret the insights. It means building new coping mechanisms and developing a stronger sense of self that can hold all of this. It's a long process, not a quick fix. And it requires immense patience and self-compassion. You're basically being reborn, and birth is messy and painful. But there's incredible potential on the other side.

Finding Your Support System

This is not something you should go through alone. You need people who either understand what's happening or, at the very least, are willing to listen without judgment. This can be hard. Most people in your regular life won't get it. They'll think you're having a mental breakdown. And honestly, from their perspective, it might look that way. So, you need to be discerning about who you share this with.

  • Kundalini Support Groups: There are online forums, Facebook groups, and sometimes even local meetups for people going through spiritual emergence or kundalini awakenings. Finding others who have walked this path can be a lifesaver. Just knowing you're not the only one can make a huge difference.
  • Spiritual Teachers/Mentors: If you can find a teacher who truly understands kundalini - not just theoretically, but from their own experience - that's invaluable. They can offer guidance, reassurance, and practical advice. Be careful here, though. There are a lot of charlatans out there. Trust your gut. If something feels off, it probably is.
  • Therapists Specializing in Spiritual Emergence: As I mentioned earlier, these professionals are trained to differentiate between mental illness and spiritual crisis. They can help you ground, process, and integrate your experiences in a safe environment.
  • A Trusted Friend or Family Member: If you have someone in your life who is open-minded and compassionate, someone you can just talk to, even if they don't fully understand, that can be a huge comfort. Sometimes just speaking about it out loud to a non-judgmental ear is enough to release some of the pressure.

Don't isolate yourself. That's one of the most dangerous things you can do during this process. The feeling of being utterly alone with these intense experiences can drive you deeper into fear and despair. Reach out. Even if it's just a message in an online group at 3 AM. Someone will be awake. Someone will understand. The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) is an older book, but it's a classic for a reason - it documents many different experiences and helps you realize you're part of a larger phenomenon. And The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) is another excellent resource for understanding this process and finding ways to cope.

Patience and Self-Compassion

This isn't a race. It's not a competition. And it's certainly not something you can force or control. Kundalini moves at its own pace, in its own way. You might have periods of intense activity followed by periods of calm, even stagnation. Don't get frustrated. Don't beat yourself up for not being "further along" or for feeling overwhelmed. This is a massive energetic and psychological overhaul. It takes time. Sometimes years. Sometimes a lifetime.

Treat yourself with the kindness you would offer a scared, vulnerable child. Because in many ways, that's what you are right now. You're confronting aspects of reality and yourself that are completely new and potentially terrifying. Give yourself permission to rest. Give yourself permission to cry. Give yourself permission to not be okay. This isn't about being strong all the time; it's about being real, being honest with yourself about what you're experiencing, and slowly, gently, learning to deal with it.

You'll have good days and bad days. You'll have moments of deep clarity and moments of utter confusion. That's all part of the process. Trust that your system is doing what it needs to do. Your job isn't to fight it, but to learn to cooperate with it, to provide it with the right conditions to unfold safely. That means grounding, seeking support, and practicing radical self-care. It means being your own best friend when the whole world feels like it's against you, or when your own mind feels like it's betraying you.

So yeah, you're not losing your mind. You're finding a new one. A bigger one. A more expansive one. It's just a really messy, uncomfortable, and frankly, scary process to get there. But you've got this. You're stronger than you think. And you're not alone. Keep breathing. Keep grounding. Keep reaching out. You'll get through this. I promise you.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
